perturbation.original_text: 1. Definition of Confidential Information 1.1. All information disclosed by the Town in oral, written, graphic, photographic, recorded, prototype, sample or in any other form that is related to the Information Technology , Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) systems for Town of The Blue Mountains or any information written, graphic, photographic, recorded, prototype, sample or in any other form that is generated by the Provider for the purpose of doing business with The Town shall be consi dered Confidential Information. Any information considered Private Information by the Municipal Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act (MFIPPA) , any personal health information as defined by the Personal Health Information Protection Act (PHIPA), and by the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA) is also considered Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Definition of Confidential Information 1.1. All information disclosed by the Town in oral or written form that is related to general business operations shall be considered Confidential Information. Any information considered Private Information by the Municipal Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act (MFIPPA) is also considered Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing specific references to Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) systems, and personal health information as defined by the Personal Health Information Protection Act (PHIPA), and by the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA) while retaining the broad statement that 'all information related to general business operations' is confidential, creates a contradiction when section 5.2 discusses access to 'personal information as defined by MFIPPA and personal health information as defined by PHIPA.' If PHIPA defined information is not included in the definition of confidential information, access to it can not be restricted.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.2. A Privacy Impact Assessment (“PIA”) shall be required if the Provider will have access to personal information as defined by MFIPPA and personal health information as defined by PHIPA, the Town sha ll have right to terminate the engagement of the Provider without any liability or penalty if the Provider fails the PIA, and the failure cannot be mitigated , within a time specified by the Town, by measures acceptable to the Town.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit inclusion of 'personal health information as defined by PHIPA' from the definition of Confidential Information, while the contradicted text (5.2) discusses the Provider having access to this same 'personal health information as defined by PHIPA'. This creates a contradiction because if PHIPA-defined information is not considered confidential under the changed text definition (1.1), it undermines the restrictions and requirements related to accessing it, as stated in section 5.2. In the original text, both were considered Confidential Information, but in the changed text, personal health information is excluded. This contradiction is strong and would cause confusion over whether access to PHIPA data requires a PIA or termination if the provider fails the PIA.

perturbation.original_text: 4.4. The Provider shall employ at all times administrative and technical security measures to the Town’s standards on access and password procedures for Provider’s personnel, encryption of Town Confidential Information while in transit and at rest, continuous monitoring of the security posture of the Information, maintenance of auditable logs including: user access logs, physical outage logs, and application logs, encryption, isolation of the Town’s Confidential Information, business continuity procedures, and provision of an encrypted method of remo te authentication and authorization.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.4. The Provider shall employ at all times reasonable security measures on access and password procedures for Provider’s personnel and maintain business continuity procedures.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific technical requirements such as 'encryption of Town Confidential Information while in transit and at rest' and 'continuous monitoring of the security posture of the Information', while retaining the general statement about 'reasonable security measures' this creates an in-text contradiction when section 4.1 refers to the Provider needing to use 'best practice security measures at all times to prevent information from being compromised.' What is considered reasonable may no longer align with the 'best practice'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.1. The Provider will agree to use their best efforts to prevent disclosure of Confidential Information to any third party for an indefinite period of time from the date of this Agreement. The Provider is to use best practice security measures at all times to prevent informati on from being compromised. All policies and procedures relating to data and information security are to be readily available to the Town from the Provider.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies detailed security measures like encryption and continuous monitoring. The changed text replaces these with 'reasonable security measures', while section 4.1 requires 'best practice security measures'. The definition of 'reasonable' is subjective and may not equate to 'best practice,' creating a contradiction and ambiguity regarding the required security standards, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1. The Town retains the right to audit the Provider to ensure that proper measures are being applied to protect any and all confidential information. Auditing may be performed by Town IT Staff or by a Third Par ty, as chosen by the Town in its sole discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1. The Town retains the right to audit the Provider, but the scope and frequency of audits will be subject to mutual agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By adding the clause 'but the scope and frequency of audits will be subject to mutual agreement,' the original intent of the Town having the right to audit to 'ensure that proper measures are being applied to protect any and all confidential information' is undermined, creating a contradiction. The effectiveness of audits to 'ensure proper measures' is now contingent on the Provider's agreement, potentially limiting the Town's ability to effectively monitor compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.1. The Town retains the right to audit the Provider, but the scope and frequency of audits will be subject to mutual agreement. Auditing may be performed by Town IT Staff or by a Third Par ty, as chosen by the Town in its sole discretion.
perturbation.justification: The original text grants the Town the right to audit the Provider. The changed text modifies this right by making the scope and frequency of audits subject to mutual agreement. This introduces a contradiction because the Town's ability to 'ensure that proper measures are being applied to protect any and all confidential information' is now dependent on the Provider's agreement, which potentially limits the Town's oversight and contradicts the initial, unrestricted right to audit. Since the changed text contains the same information as the Contradicted Text, it still causes a contradiction, and is an omission based in-text contradiction as defined above.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Return of Confidential I nformation 6.1. The Town may, at any time, request the return of all Confidential Information (including notes generated by the Provider on Confidential Information given orally) and all copies thereof, received from or on behalf of the Town , and the Provider agrees to promptly comply with such requests. The Provider agrees that, subsequent to a request for return of Confidential Information or notification of termination of business discussions and/or collaboration, Confidential Information provided orally will continue to be kept confidential by the Provider and the provisions of this Agreement shall continue with respect to all Confidential Information until an y of items 3.1 a), b) and c) become applicable. If the information is unable to be returned then information must be destroyed and a certificate of destruction must be issued.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Return of Confidential I nformation 6.1. The Town may, at any time, request the return of all Confidential Information received from or on behalf of the Town, and the Provider agrees to comply with such requests. If the information is unable to be returned then information may be destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the phrase '(including notes generated by the Provider on Confidential Information given orally) and all copies thereof' from the requirement to return Confidential Information, while retaining the obligation to keep Confidential Information provided orally confidential (as stated in the next sentence), creates a contradiction. The Provider is obligated to keep orally provided information confidential, but they are no longer explicitly required to return notes or copies of that information. This creates uncertainty about how the confidentiality of orally provided information is maintained if the notes/copies are not returned or destroyed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. Return of Confidential I nformation 6.1. The Town may, at any time, request the return of all Confidential Information received from or on behalf of the Town, and the Provider agrees to comply with such requests. The Provider agrees that, subsequent to a request for return of Confidential Information or notification of termination of business discussions and/or collaboration, Confidential Information provided orally will continue to be kept confidential by the Provider and the provisions of this Agreement shall continue with respect to all Confidential Information until an y of items 3.1 a), b) and c) become applicable. If the information is unable to be returned then information may be destroyed.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the return of 'all Confidential Information (including notes generated by the Provider on Confidential Information given orally) and all copies thereof'. The changed text omits '(including notes generated by the Provider on Confidential Information given orally) and all copies thereof' but retains the requirement to keep orally provided information confidential. The contradicted text includes both provisions. The omission in the changed text creates a contradiction because it implies the notes and copies of orally provided information are no longer subject to the return requirement, despite the ongoing confidentiality obligation. This creates a strong inconsistency and unenforceability on how orally provided information is maintained, as defined by the Omissions - In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Length of Agreement 11.1. This agreement shall be in full force upon its execution by the Parties and shall be and effect inde finitely. For greater clarity and certainty, the obligations herein with respect to Confidential Information continue indefinitely and beyond the time limit of the specific engagement for whi ch the Town engaged the Provider .
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Length of Agreement 11.1. This agreement shall be in full force upon its execution by the Parties and shall be in effect for the duration of the engagement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the agreement's length to 'the duration of the engagement', the original text's explicit statement that 'the obligations herein with respect to Confidential Information continue indefinitely and beyond the time limit of the specific engagement' is directly contradicted. This creates a significant contradiction concerning the longevity of the confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11. Length of Agreement 11.1. This agreement shall be in full force upon its execution by the Parties and shall be in effect for the duration of the engagement. For greater clarity and certainty, the obligations herein with respect to Confidential Information continue indefinitely and beyond the time limit of the specific engagement for whi ch the Town engaged the Provider .
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that confidentiality obligations continue indefinitely, even beyond the engagement period. The changed text limits the agreement's duration to 'the duration of the engagement.' This directly contradicts the earlier statement about the indefinite continuation of confidentiality obligations, creating a significant in-text contradiction. The Contradicted Text section highlights this as well, as it shows the contradiction in the same section. Therefore, the modification introduces a clear conflict, warranting a 'YES'.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2. The Provider shall protect the Town ’s Confidential Information in accordance with applicable privacy legislation .
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2. The Provider shall protect the Town’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'in accordance with applicable privacy legislation' from the obligation to protect confidential information creates an in-text contradiction with Section 10.1, which states 'This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with all applicable laws and by-laws of the Town, Province of Ontario and any applicable Canadian federal laws.' Without the specific requirement to adhere to privacy legislation in Section 4.2, it introduces ambiguity as to whether the general governing laws clause in Section 10.1 sufficiently enforces privacy law compliance, since it's not specified.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10.1. This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with all applicable laws and by -laws of the Town, Province of Ontario and any applicable Canadian federal laws.
perturbation.justification: The original text of Section 4.2 required the Provider to protect the Town's Confidential Information 'in accordance with applicable privacy legislation'. The changed text removes this explicit requirement. Section 10.1 states the agreement is governed by all applicable laws. The removal of the explicit mention of privacy legislation creates a contradiction because it is unclear whether the general governance clause (10.1) is sufficient to enforce privacy law compliance, given that it was explicitly stated in the original Section 4.2. This creates ambiguity and thus a contradiction.
